title: Psalms 126
subtitle: When the LORD Restored the Fortunes of Zion

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “Our Mouths Were Filled with Laughter”

heb.paragraph: "When the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion, we were like those who dreamed. Our mouths were filled with laughter (səḥōq), our tongues with songs of joy" (vv.1–2). The restoration was so unexpected it felt like a dream. Laughter and singing erupted spontaneously. Even the nations noticed: "The LORD has done great things for them" (v.2). The community confirms: "The LORD has done great things for us, and we are filled with joy" (v.3).

notes.note: "We were like those who DREAMED. Our mouths were filled with LAUGHTER." The restoration was surreal — too good to be real. Joy broke out before analysis could begin.

"Then it was said among the nations, ‘The LORD has done great things for them.’ The LORD has done great things for US." Even outsiders recognised God’s work. The testimony is global before it is personal.

sections.header: Verses 4–6 — “Those Who Sow in Tears Will Reap with Songs of Joy”

heb.paragraph: "Those who sow with tears (dimʿâ) will reap with songs of joy. Those who go out weeping, carrying seed to sow, will return with songs of joy, carrying sheaves with them" (vv.5–6). The psalm’s most quoted verses. The agricultural metaphor: tears are seed. Weeping is sowing. Joy is harvest. The farmer goes out crying and comes back singing. The proportion is radical: tears produce songs; weeping produces sheaves. Time transforms sorrow into celebration.

notes.note: "Restore our fortunes, LORD, like streams in the Negev." The Negev’s dry wadis suddenly flood with winter rain. Restoration can be sudden and overwhelming.
"Those who SOW in TEARS will REAP with SONGS of joy." The sequence: sowing (labour) + tears (suffering) → reaping (harvest) + songs (joy). Weeping is not wasted; it is planting.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Lord has done great things for us, and we are filled with joy.ESVThe LORD has done great things for us; we are glad.NIVOur mouths were filled with laughter, our tongues with songs of joy. Then it was said among the nations, “The Lord has done great things for them.”ESVThen our mouth was filled with laughter, and our tongue with shouts of joy; then they said among the nations, \u201cThe LORD has done great things for them.\u201d

rec.text: ""We were like those who DREAMED. Our mouths were filled with LAUGHTER." The restoration was surreal — too good to be real. Joy broke out before analysis could begin."

rows.label: Dreaming and Laughing — 126:1–3
rows.text: "We were like those who DREAMED." Mouths filled with laughter. Even the nations noticed.

hebtext.note: "When the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion, we were like those who dreamed. Our mouths were filled with laughter (səḥōq), our tongues with songs of joy" (vv.1–2). The restoration was so unexpected it felt like a dream. Laughter and singing erupted spontaneously. Even the nations noticed: "The LORD has done great things for them" (v.2). The community confirms: "The LORD has done great things for us, and we are filled with joy" (v.3).

"Those who sow with tears (dimʿâ) will reap with songs of joy. Those who go out weeping, carrying seed to sow, will return with songs of joy, carrying sheaves with them" (vv.5–6). The psalm’s most quoted verses. The agricultural metaphor: tears are seed. Weeping is sowing. Joy is harvest. The farmer goes out crying and comes back singing. The proportion is radical: tears produce songs; weeping produces sheaves. Time transforms sorrow into celebration.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: tears transformed to joy. The sowing-reaping metaphor is the psalm’s enduring theology. Prophecy: restoration language applicable to every exile and return.
